sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Eleanor Matthews 
St Bartholomew's Hospital 
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE 

Dear Dr Eleanor Matthews, 

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1989, Hospital No: H392745 NHS No. 9034512786 
Flat 3, Willow Supported Living, 22 Brook Lane, London, SE15 4JG 

Diagnoses: 

Generalised epilepsy with documented generalised spike-and-wave activity on prior EEG (2017)

sections.name: current medication
sections.text:
Sodium valproate 500 mg bd 
Clobazam 10 mg nocte (as rescue on cluster days)

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily 
- Maintain clobazam 10 mg at night for up to 3 nights during cluster periods, as per existing protocol 
- Continue seizure charting by supported accommodation staff and share monthly summaries 
- Consider ambulatory EEG if frequency or pattern worsens 
- Safety advice reinforced; SUDEP risk discussed; emphasis on sleep regularity and alcohol avoidance 

I reviewed Michael Turner in my clinic today. He lives in supported accommodation where rotating staff maintain daily seizure charts; these charts, which I reviewed, are used as our primary source of frequency data. Over the last four weeks, the records note: "Cluster days twice this month; typically six seizures in 24 h." These events are brief generalised convulsions with rapid recovery, no new focal features, and no injuries reported. Staff have been consistent in logging times, witnessed features, and rescue medication use. 

He describes a prodrome of diffuse head pressure and photophobia on days preceding clusters, occasionally with poor sleep the night before. There is no clear relation to intercurrent illness, but stress and missed meals were mentioned as possible triggers. He reports good adherence to sodium valproate, and staff confirm administration records align. Where rescue has been needed, clobazam 10 mg nocte has been used for up to three consecutive nights with good effect and without excessive daytime sedation. 

Neurologically he is unchanged on examination. Weight stable, no tremor, and no rash. Liver function tests in August were within normal limits; we will repeat monitoring at the usual interval. He remains seizure‑free between clusters for prolonged stretches, and there have been no emergency department attendances this year. 

We discussed options. Given the current balance of efficacy and tolerability, I recommend continuing sodium valproate at the present dose, with staff continuing meticulous charting. If clusters increase in frequency or if daytime sedation from clobazam becomes problematic, we can consider either a small valproate titration or introducing lamotrigine as adjunct. For now, no immediate changes are required. 

I have provided updated written guidance for staff regarding when to initiate clobazam on cluster days, thresholds for calling 999, and parameters for documenting duration and post‑ictal recovery (Ã‚Â· please file in the medication folder). Standard safety advice was reiterated, including supervision when bathing, avoiding heights, and maintaining sleep hygiene. We also reviewed SUDEP information briefly and gave a leaflet for the team and key worker. 

Follow‑up: I will review him in six months, sooner if the pattern shifts (e.g. more than two cluster days per month or any prolonged seizure >5 minutes). The team will continue to email monthly seizure chart summaries to our clinic address. 

With best wishes, 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 8 September 2010 

Dr Patel
Elmwood Medical Centre

72 Elmwood Road, London, SE5 3HT
Dear Dr Patel
Jane Smith, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: H738291 NHS No. 0093384721
Flat 4, 21 Brook Lane, London, SE5 1AB 

Thank you for asking me to review the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. She is known to our service with focal epilepsy and, encouragingly, reports a steady improvement over recent months, which she attributes to better adherence following use of a consistent seizure diary and reminders on her phone.

Current treatment consists of Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d., both well tolerated without dose-limiting adverse effects. She reports improved energy levels compared to earlier in the year and no new intercurrent illnesses. There is no current concern regarding mood or sleep beyond occasional pre-examination anxiety, which she manages with standard breathing techniques taught by our epilepsy nurse specialist.

Regarding recent events: In February she experienced a prolonged focal seizure lasting 8 minutes, resolving without intervention. In May there were four further brief absences, and in August a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure was reported at school (not witnessed by family). These episodes have been clearly recorded in her diary, with triggers likely related to missed meals and sleep deprivation around coursework deadlines. Since adopting structured reminders in late August, she has had no further events and has taken all doses on time.

Neurological examination today is unremarkable. There are no drug-related rashes, no tremor, and no cognitive concerns on bedside screening. We discussed safety and rescue planning; no rescue medication has been required. She prefers to avoid changes while her control is improving, and I agree that stability is prudent given the recent positive trajectory.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. unchanged.
- Maintain seizure diary and phone reminders; continue avoidance of sleep deprivation and missed meals.
- Bloods today for routine monitoring (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) – to be copied to you.
- Review in clinic in 4 months, sooner if breakthrough seizures recur or if tolerability issues arise.

Overall, her focal epilepsy appears better controlled with improved adherence, and she is optimistic about maintaining this trajectory.

Yours sincerely

citations.quote: Regarding recent events: In February she experienced a prolonged focal seizure lasting 8 minutes, resolving without intervention. In May there were four further brief absences, and in August a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure was reported at school (not witnessed by family). These episodes have been clearly recorded in her diary, with triggers likely related to missed meals and sleep deprivation around coursework deadlines. Since adopting structured reminders in late August, she has had no further events and has taken all doses on time.
Current treatment consists of Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d.

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Thomas Reid (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 14 October 2019 10:15
To: neuro.epilepsy@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Meera Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Patrick O’Neill (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Telephone review and travel-related considerations

Dear Thomas

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Patient has generalised epilepsy.

Present Medication:

1. Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for jet-lag periods (patient-reported intermittent use)

sections.name: present seizure frequency
sections.text:
Two events over the last five months.

History and Context:

This is a follow-up conducted by telephone as he is a frequent business traveller with irregular sleep and time zones. He reports adherence to valproate on most days but occasionally misses doses during long-haul flights. His first seizure occurred in May 2019 in Ireland, at night while asleep. He woke with rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a sense of déjà vu. The second event was in October 2019 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms. He denies daytime collapses, injuries, or prolonged postictal confusion. No alcohol excess reported; significant sleep disruption around both events.

Plan of Action:

- Reinforced general measures for generalised epilepsy with emphasis on strict medication adherence when crossing time zones; advised use of alarms and written dosing plans for flights.
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily. He prefers no change at present given infrequency of events and travel schedule.
- May use clobazam 10 mg at night for up to three nights around major time-zone shifts if sleep disruption is expected, as previously advised.
- Arrange routine EEG at next in-person visit when in London; if logistics difficult, consider ambulatory EEG coordinated around his travel.
- Safety counselling provided (no driving until compliant with DVLA guidance; avoid sleep deprivation, swimming alone, or climbing heights).
- Next review by telephone in three months or sooner if further seizures occur.

With best wishes,

sections.name: body
sections.text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 15 September 2023 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist 
King's College Hospital 
Department of Neurosciences, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist, 

James Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH472918 NHS No. 9473165820 
Flat 12, 8 Millwright Lane, London, SE15 3AB

Thank you for your referral. I reviewed the patient in clinic following concerns about ongoing seizures in the context of his work with metal fabrication and intermittent arc-light exposure. He reports strict adherence to workplace safety practices, including a full protective welding visor and gloves, and says he does not remove the visor during active welding. He is otherwise in good general health. There is no recent history of head injury, meningitis, or alcohol misuse. Sleep has been variable due to shift changes at the workshop.

Regarding current events, he still has focal onset seizures four times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. He and his partner describe the focal episodes as brief lapses with a rising internal sensation and impaired awareness lasting under a minute, often clustering late morning. The drop attacks tend to occur in short runs over two to three days, typically in the early evening after work, with quick recovery but occasional bruising. The tonic-clonic events are usually nocturnal or in the early hours; he is sore and fatigued for the following day. He denies clear photic triggers, and states the seizure frequency does not vary with welding versus non-welding days. He continues his regular medication regimen as previously prescribed and has not missed doses.

He does not drive and remains in manual employment with modified duties when fatigued. His employer is aware of his condition and has implemented buddy supervision during hot-work tasks. There have been no burns or occupational incidents attributable to events on the shop floor. He reports that hydration, regular breaks, and consistent sleep help him feel steadier, though not changing the overall frequency. No new neurological symptoms were volunteered. Examination in clinic today was unremarkable with normal speech, cranial nerves, tone, and coordination; gait steady.

I have advised ongoing documentation of events with dates, times, and context (workday versus rest day, sleep duration, and recent stressors), and asked him to bring his workplace risk assessment to the next appointment so that we can ensure appropriate safety planning remains in place. We also discussed continuing to avoid working at heights or near unguarded machinery during periods of increased events. He is agreeable to sharing seizure logs with your practice.

I will arrange routine follow-up and will update you if any new information emerges from collateral reports or monitoring. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Amelia Rhodes
Riverside Medical Practice
14 Brook Street, London W1A 3QL

Dear Dr Amelia Rhodes,

Alex Morgan, DOB: 22-08-1991, Hospital No: U918273 NHS No. 3021187745
Flat 3, 7 Harrow Lane, London NW1 5TS

Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with photosensitivity and startle-triggered events; early-years childcare worker in a high-noise, multitasking environment; sleep fragmentation due to interrupted midday rest breaks; history of mild anxiety symptoms related to workplace interruptions.

Medication: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily.

I reviewed the patient in clinic today. They attended alone. They report ongoing challenges at work in a nursery setting where the ambient noise is high, tasks require frequent rapid switching of attention, and planned midday rest breaks are often interrupted by staffing needs. Over the past three months they describe several episodes per week of brief generalised events, typically characterised by abrupt behavioural arrest with eyelid fluttering and loss of awareness lasting 10–20 seconds, occasionally clustering in the early afternoon when rest has been curtailed. Colleagues have noted brief pauses and dropped tasks without warning, followed by rapid recovery. There have been two morning episodes with sudden generalised jerks on awakening, leading to the patient dropping lightweight items but without injury.

The patient has no focal aura or lateralising features. No tongue biting or sustained postictal confusion has been reported after the brief absence-type events. Sleep is curtailed by early starts and the unreliable rest period at midday. Alcohol is minimal, and there is no recreational drug use. They deny missed doses; a weekly pill organiser is used. There have been no recent infections or intercurrent illnesses.

Examination today was unremarkable with normal cardiovascular and neurological findings. Weight and blood pressure are within target range. Review of prior investigations: MRI brain (2023) was normal. EEG (2024) showed generalised 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges provoked by hyperventilation and intermittent photic stimulation, consistent with generalised epilepsy. Routine bloods last month were normal including FBC and LFTs.

Seizures: Patient currently experiences several episodes per week of brief generalised absence-type seizures, with occasional morning myoclonic jerks on awakening. These episodes are more frequent on days when the midday rest break is interrupted.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Diagnosis reaffirmed as generalised epilepsy. Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily for now; adherence is good and no adverse effects reported.
- Provide seizure diary template to document timing relative to rest break interruptions and high-noise periods at work.
- Workplace liaison letter (with patient consent) offering neutral information about generalised epilepsy and recommending predictable, uninterrupted short rest period where feasible, and avoidance of strobe-like lighting in play areas.
- Safety advice reiterated for childcare duties: ensure another adult supervises during water play or ladder use; avoid carrying hot liquids during early morning periods when myoclonic jerks have occurred.
- Consider dose optimisation or alternative broad-spectrum therapy if diary confirms persistent several episodes per week despite stable routine; review in 3 months with diary and, if needed, repeat EEG with sleep deprivation.

Yours sincerely,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Priya Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 17 June 2020 10:12
To: kch.epilepsyclinic@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Michael Turner (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Emily Chen (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Review and medication adjustment

Dear Dr Shah,

Epilepsy diagnosis:

Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, based on semiology and diary-linked patterns.

Present medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed (intermittent use per patient)

sections.name: present seizure frequency
sections.text:
According to the patient’s seizure diary app, events have clustered around sudden noises; over the past six weeks there were four brief focal-aware episodes and one focal-impaired awareness spell, typically occurring when exposed to abrupt auditory shocks (e.g., dropped crockery or unexpected alarms). The diary time-stamps correlate with environments of heightened noise and suggest a pattern consistent with startle-triggered focal events rather than spontaneous occurrences.

Plan of action:

- We discussed optimising treatment for focal seizures with sensory triggers. The patient reports good adherence and stable lamotrigine serum levels previously. Given the diary-documented noise-precipitated events and occasional breakthrough focal-impaired awareness, I recommend adding lacosamide as adjunctive therapy while maintaining lamotrigine.

- Initiate lacosamide as follows: 50 mg at night for 1 week, then 50 mg twice daily for 1 week, then increase to 100 mg in the morning and 50 mg at night for 1 week, and thereafter 100 mg twice daily if tolerated. Continue this dose until review.

- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily unchanged for now. Clobazam may remain as intermittent rescue for anticipated high-risk noise exposures, but we advised minimising routine use to avoid sedation.

- The patient will continue to use the seizure diary app to log events, noting context (sound level, location, preceding stress, sleep quality) and any medication side effects. We asked them to enable the app’s noise-trigger tagging feature to refine correlation analysis.

- Safety: advised on avoiding hazardous activities when episodes are more likely (e.g., handling hot liquids during known noisy times). No driving until seizure-free in accordance with DVLA guidance; we have reiterated current restrictions and the need to report any loss of awareness events.

Follow-up:

- Please arrange basic metabolic panel and ECG locally before uptitration to 100 mg twice daily to screen for any conduction concerns.
- I have scheduled a review in 4 months. If well-tolerated with improved control, we will consider cautious clobazam de-escalation. If breakthrough events persist despite lacosamide, we will discuss alternative adjuncts or video-EEG monitoring.

With best wishes,

sections.name: body
sections.text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Sharma
Consultant Neurologist 
King's College Hospital 
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr A. Sharma
Consultant Neurologist, 

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K912345 NHS No. 9076543211 
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 3QT

Thank you for your referral. I reviewed Daniel Carter in clinic today with his partner present. Unfortunately, there has been a clear deterioration over recent months, marked by an increase in seizure-related incidents and several episodes resulting in falls and minor injuries (forearm bruising and a superficial eyebrow laceration managed with steri-strips at a walk-in centre). He describes focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and left arm tingling, followed by behavioural arrest, lip-smacking, and unresponsiveness for 60–90 seconds. Post-event confusion lasts around 20–30 minutes, often with headache and fatigue.

Frequency has escalated. He and his partner report that what were previously sporadic episodes have evolved into short bursts of activity linked to warm environments and sustained physical activity—particularly on school field afternoons and community fun-run days—after which he may have two to three events over a 24–48 hour period. Outside these activity-linked bursts, he is now averaging one focal impaired-awareness seizure every 7–10 days. No clear nocturnal convulsive events have been witnessed, though he wakes on some mornings with tongue bite indentations and myalgia suggestive of possible secondary generalisation overnight on two occasions in the last six weeks.

He has focal epilepsy. Onset was in 2019 following a mild traumatic head injury. MRI (2021) demonstrated right mesial temporal T2/FLAIR signal change without clear volume loss; EEG (2022) showed right temporal interictal sharp waves. Current anti-seizure medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily and Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily, both reportedly taken reliably. He notes mood lability on Levetiracetam but prefers not to alter this while seizures are active. There is no recent alcohol excess; sleep has been fragmented due to childcare demands. No intercurrent infection reported.

Given the recent falls and injury risk, coupled with the apparent cluster pattern associated with warm, exertional community events, I am concerned about a worsening of seizure control. I have requested an urgent repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and ambulatory EEG (72 hours) to capture both habitual focal events and any sleep-related activity. I have also issued a safety plan, including advice on hydration, gradual warm-up/cool-down for physical activity, and supervision during higher-risk periods, as discussed. He has agreed to track events using a diary and smartphone seizure app, including antecedents, duration, and recovery features.

I will review him in four weeks or sooner if further injuries occur. Depending on results, we may consider transitioning from Levetiracetam to Brivaracetam or adding Lacosamide, but I will defer a firm decision until investigations are complete. I have given interim written guidance to his workplace regarding temporary duty modification to reduce risk from ladders and hot environments. DVLA advice has been reiterated in line with current seizure activity.

Please let me know if there are any additional concerns or if further information emerges from primary care. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: body
sections.text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Sharma
Consultant Neurologist 
King's College Hospital 
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr A. Sharma
Consultant Neurologist, 

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K912345 NHS No. 9076543211 
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 3QT

Thank you for your referral. I reviewed Daniel Carter in clinic today with his partner present. Unfortunately, there has been a clear deterioration over recent months, marked by an increase in seizure-related incidents and several episodes resulting in falls and minor injuries (forearm bruising and a superficial eyebrow laceration managed with steri-strips at a walk-in centre). He describes focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and left arm tingling, followed by behavioural arrest, lip-smacking, and unresponsiveness for 60–90 seconds. Post-event confusion lasts around 20–30 minutes, often with headache and fatigue.

Frequency has escalated. He and his partner report that what were previously sporadic episodes have evolved into short bursts of activity linked to warm environments and sustained physical activity—particularly on school field afternoons and community fun-run days—after which he may have two to three events over a 24–48 hour period. Outside these activity-linked bursts, he is now averaging one focal impaired-awareness seizure every 7–10 days. No clear nocturnal convulsive events have been witnessed, though he wakes on some mornings with tongue bite indentations and myalgia suggestive of possible secondary generalisation overnight on two occasions in the last six weeks.

He has focal epilepsy. Onset was in 2019 following a mild traumatic head injury. MRI (2021) demonstrated right mesial temporal T2/FLAIR signal change without clear volume loss; EEG (2022) showed right temporal interictal sharp waves. Current anti-seizure medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily and Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily, both reportedly taken reliably. He notes mood lability on Levetiracetam but prefers not to alter this while seizures are active. There is no recent alcohol excess; sleep has been fragmented due to childcare demands. No intercurrent infection reported.

Given the recent falls and injury risk, coupled with the apparent cluster pattern associated with warm, exertional community events, I am concerned about a worsening of seizure control. I have requested an urgent repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and ambulatory EEG (72 hours) to capture both habitual focal events and any sleep-related activity. I have also issued a safety plan, including advice on hydration, gradual warm-up/cool-down for physical activity, and supervision during higher-risk periods, as discussed. He has agreed to track events using a diary and smartphone seizure app, including antecedents, duration, and recovery features.

I will review him in four weeks or sooner if further injuries occur. Depending on results, we may consider transitioning from Levetiracetam to Brivaracetam or adding Lacosamide, but I will defer a firm decision until investigations are complete. I have given interim written guidance to his workplace regarding temporary duty modification to reduce risk from ladders and hot environments. DVLA advice has been reiterated in line with current seizure activity.

Please let me know if there are any additional concerns or if further information emerges from primary care. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Ahmed 
St Mary's Hospital 
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY 

Dear Dr Ahmed, 

Sophie Daniels, DOB: 12-05-1991, Hospital No: H948320 NHS No. 8934751206 
14 Brookside Close, London, W10 6JP

sections.name: medication
sections.text:
Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Patient reports tolerating this without clear adverse effects apart from occasional fatigue. No recent dose changes.

History since last review: 

She describes intermittent nocturnal episodes over the past three months with post-event confusion on waking and occasional tongue-biting reported by her partner. She remains currently not driving by self-report. Clusters characterized by two focal impaired-awareness seizures; frequency unclear. She is keeping a basic diary but cannot specify how often these occur, as some events may be unwitnessed.

She notes increased stress at work and irregular sleep. No intercurrent illness, head injury, or new medications. Caffeine intake modest; no alcohol excess. Menses regular; no clear cyclical pattern noted by her. Family is supportive and monitoring overnight. No emergency department attendances. 

Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, gait, and cranial nerve examination. Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily for now. 
- Implement structured seizure and trigger diary with partner corroboration to help clarify timing and frequency of clusters and any precipitants (stress, sleep loss). 
- Safety counselling reiterated, including bathing precautions and avoidance of heights or open water when alone. She remains off driving and understands to inform DVLA as appropriate per current guidance. 
- Arrange routine EEG and updated serum biochemistry including U&E, LFT, and levetiracetam level (for reference only). 
- Provide information on sleep hygiene and stress management resources; patient agreeable. 
- We will review in clinic in 4 months or sooner if events escalate, any injury occurs, or if there is a change in pattern. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: medication
sections.text:
Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Patient reports tolerating this without clear adverse effects apart from occasional fatigue. No recent dose changes.

History since last review: 

She describes intermittent nocturnal episodes over the past three months with post-event confusion on waking and occasional tongue-biting reported by her partner. She remains currently not driving by self-report. Clusters characterized by two focal impaired-awareness seizures; frequency unclear. She is keeping a basic diary but cannot specify how often these occur, as some events may be unwitnessed.

She notes increased stress at work and irregular sleep. No intercurrent illness, head injury, or new medications. Caffeine intake modest; no alcohol excess. Menses regular; no clear cyclical pattern noted by her. Family is supportive and monitoring overnight. No emergency department attendances. 

Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, gait, and cranial nerve examination. Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily for now. 
- Implement structured seizure and trigger diary with partner corroboration to help clarify timing and frequency of clusters and any precipitants (stress, sleep loss). 
- Safety counselling reiterated, including bathing precautions and avoidance of heights or open water when alone. She remains off driving and understands to inform DVLA as appropriate per current guidance. 
- Arrange routine EEG and updated serum biochemistry including U&E, LFT, and levetiracetam level (for reference only). 
- Provide information on sleep hygiene and stress management resources; patient agreeable. 
- We will review in clinic in 4 months or sooner if events escalate, any injury occurs, or if there is a change in pattern. 

Yours sincerely

Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

Subject: Review and medication adjustment

Hi Emily

Diagnosis remains under review with ongoing characterisation. No definitive electroclinical classification assigned at present.

Present Medication:

1. Sodium valproate 300 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam 5 mg at night

sections.name: present seizure frequency
sections.text:
The parents state that since starting ketogenic diet he has had 4 drop attacks, the latest one on 17 Dec. They report no clear precipitating triggers; sleep is variable due to the family currently awaiting relocation from temporary accommodation, with crowded living conditions noted and frequent nocturnal disturbances.

Plan of Action:

- Continue ketogenic diet with dietician oversight; parents to maintain seizure and dietary logs and report any concerns regarding hydration or tolerance.
- Increase clobazam to 5 mg in the morning and 5 mg at night for two weeks, then 10 mg at night thereafter if daytime sedation occurs; maintain sodium valproate at current dose pending review of response and tolerability.
- Arrange ambulatory EEG within the next 6–8 weeks to assist with classification and to capture typical events if possible.
- Refer to hospital family liaison for support regarding housing-related stressors that may be impacting sleep and seizure threshold.
- Safety advice reiterated regarding drop attacks: use of protective headgear during high‑risk activities and avoidance of unsupervised bathing.
- Follow‑up in three months or sooner if attacks cluster or there is any injury.

With best wishes

sections.name: body
sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 12 October 2019 

Dr Patel 
St Cuthbert’s NHS Trust 
Neurology Department, 12 Waverley Road, Bristol BS1 4QF 

Dear Dr Patel, 

Jordan Ellis, DOB: 14-11-1988, Hospital No: H392174 NHS No. 9081764521 
Flat 3, 29 Brookfield Terrace, Bristol BS7 1NL 

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset. 

I reviewed Jordan Ellis in the epilepsy clinic today. They remain on long-term antiseizure therapy, with bone-health monitoring recorded elsewhere. They report stereotyped focal aware episodes characterised by a brief rising epigastric sensation and déjà vu, occasionally progressing to impaired awareness with automatisms lasting 1–2 minutes, followed by mild post-ictal fatigue. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported. Seizure clustering is not described.

Since the last review, there has been no intercurrent illness, sleep pattern is stable, and there is no clear relation to menses or alcohol. Work attendance is good and no injuries have occurred. Importantly, the median inter-seizure interval ≈ six weeks, with the patient noting that stress and missed meals can shorten this interval, while regular routines appear protective.

Neurological examination today was non-focal. No new adverse effects from medication were reported. Driving status and SUDEP risk were discussed; no red-flag features such as prolonged impaired awareness, cyanosis, or trauma were reported. Emergency care plans remain unchanged as seizures self-terminate within minutes and there has been no requirement for rescue medication.

We agreed to continue current management and to keep a detailed seizure diary capturing potential precipitants, duration, and level of awareness during events. Investigations and bone-health reviews are being coordinated through existing pathways and are documented elsewhere. We will plan routine follow-up in six months, or earlier should there be a change in frequency, semiology, or recovery profile.

Yours sincerely

They report stereotyped focal aware episodes characterised by a brief rising epigastric sensation and déjà vu, occasionally progressing to impaired awareness with automatisms lasting 1–2 minutes, followed by mild post-ictal fatigue. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported. Seizure clustering is not described.

Since the last review, there has been no intercurrent illness, sleep pattern is stable, and there is no clear relation to menses or alcohol. Work attendance is good and no injuries have occurred. Importantly, the median inter-seizure interval ≈ six weeks, with the patient noting that stress and missed meals can shorten this interval, while regular routines appear protective.

Neurological examination today was non-focal. No new adverse effects from medication were reported. Driving status and SUDEP risk were discussed; no red-flag features such as prolonged impaired awareness, cyanosis, or trauma were reported. Emergency care plans remain unchanged as seizures self-terminate within minutes and there has been no requirement for rescue medication.

We agreed to continue current management and to keep a detailed seizure diary capturing potential precipitants, duration, and level of awareness during events. Investigations and bone-health reviews are being coordinated through existing pathways and are documented elsewhere. We will plan routine follow-up in six months, or earlier should there be a change in frequency, semiology, or recovery profile.

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 15 April 2015

Dr James Rowe
Stour Vale Medical Centre

14 Millstream Way
Stourbridge
West Midlands DY8 1QG
Dear Dr James Rowe
Mr Daniel Kerr, DOB: 03-02-1986, Hospital No: H728915 NHS No. 7410592264
42 Forge Mews, Unit 6, West Bromwich, B70 8QR 

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures of unclear classification; prior routine EEG reported as non-diagnostic; past MRI (2012) described as structurally unremarkable without focal lesion. Driving suspended and remains under DVLA self-report guidance. Occupational exposure: metalwork/welding with intermittent arc-light exposure; uses protective visor per workplace routine. 

Previous medication: Levetiracetam (behavioural irritability), Sodium Valproate (weight gain, tremor), Carbamazepine (dizziness), Lamotrigine (ineffective at therapeutic dose), Clobazam (recent withdrawal). Rescue: Buccal midazolam supplied previously, unused in the past year. 

Not taken: Topiramate, Zonisamide, Lacosamide, Perampanel, Pregabalin, Tiagabine, Rufinamide. 

I reviewed him in clinic today, attended alone. He works full-time as a welder/fabricator and reports good adherence to workplace safety, including visor and appropriate filters; no sustained photic provocation at work. He notes regular sleep and reduced caffeine. He withdrew from Clobazam on 12/Jan. At that time, he had 2 seizures, one precipitated by illness. He has remained stable without seizures since. He reports occasional brief light-headedness under stress but no witnessed events, no tongue-biting, no incontinence, and no post-ictal confusion since January. No new injuries.

sections.name: current medication
sections.text:
- None for seizure prophylaxis at present
- Thiamine 100 mg daily (self-initiated)
- Omeprazole 20 mg once daily for dyspepsia 

Work/occupational context: Continues welding duties; avoids lone working for heavy lifts; colleagues aware of emergency plan. Reports no issues with arc flash given visor use; no sustained photic discomfort. He drives neither privately nor professionally. 

Examination: BP 124/76, pulse 72 regular. Neurological examination normal; no focal deficit. Fundoscopy normal (no papilloedema). Gait normal. 

Investigations: Prior EEG (2014) non-specific. MRI brain (2012) structurally normal. Routine bloods today (FBC, U&E, LFT, B12, folate, TFTs) requested. 

Discussion and plan:
- Given his current stability off Clobazam since January with no further seizures, we agreed to continue observation without initiating a new anti-seizure medication at this point. We discussed red flags and early contact plan. 
- I advised continued adherence to workplace safety standards; he already uses appropriate visor/filters; no change required. 
- To maintain a seizure diary (including intercurrent illness, sleep disruption, and any welding shift variations). 
- If breakthrough events occur, we discussed possible future options (e.g. Lacosamide or Zonisamide) and the need to consider side-effect profiles relevant to concentration and visual focus at work. 
- DVLA guidance reiterated; he confirms ongoing compliance. 
- Follow-up in 4–6 months, earlier if any recurrence. Telephone review acceptable. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Priya Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 18 February 2018 10:15
To: kch.neuroclinic@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Michael O’Connor (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Helen Davies (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Worsening seizure control and access barriers at work

Dear Priya,

Diagnosis:

Seizure disorder of uncertain classification.

Current Medicines:

1. Carbamazepine modified‑release 400 mg twice daily
2. Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily

Seizure Course and Triggers:

The patient reports a clear deterioration over the past two months. Skipping meals triggers focal epileptic spasms, and this has become more frequent due to their shift pattern and restricted access to personal items at a cleanroom/data‑hall workplace. They describe brief clusters of spasms lasting seconds, sometimes progressing to confusion if a full dose has been missed. Workplace controls have delayed retrieval of rescue medication and refills on several occasions, leading to missed doses and a noticeable worsening of seizure control compared with earlier this year.

Context and Contributing Factors:

- The patient must request escorted access to lockers and pharmacy deliveries, often resulting in delays beyond dosing times.
- Meal breaks are occasionally curtailed or rescheduled at short notice during maintenance windows, coinciding with increased spasms on fasted days.
- No alcohol or recreational drug use reported; sleep is fragmented on night shifts.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Provide the patient with a written dosing schedule and a work letter requesting reasonable adjustments: guaranteed meal breaks aligned with dosing, timely escorted access to personal medication, and permission to carry a limited supply of non‑contaminating, sealed medication within permitted zones.
- Issue a duplicate supply card for community pharmacy to reduce refill delays and encourage use of a monitored dosage system to mitigate missed doses.
- Maintain current doses for now while the above adjustments are implemented; we will reassess frequency and severity at review in 8–10 weeks.
- If access and adherence improve but spasms persist, we will discuss alternative therapies at next clinic visit.

I have arranged follow‑up in the next available clinic. Please advise if workplace liaison documentation requires specific wording.

With best wishes,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Priya Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 18 February 2018 10:15
To: kch.neuroclinic@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Michael O’Connor (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Helen Davies (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Worsening seizure control and access barriers at work

Dear Priya,

Diagnosis:

Seizure disorder of uncertain classification.

Current Medicines:

1. Carbamazepine modified‑release 400 mg twice daily
2. Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily

Seizure Course and Triggers:

The patient reports a clear deterioration over the past two months. Skipping meals triggers focal epileptic spasms, and this has become more frequent due to their shift pattern and restricted access to personal items at a cleanroom/data‑hall workplace. They describe brief clusters of spasms lasting seconds, sometimes progressing to confusion if a full dose has been missed. Workplace controls have delayed retrieval of rescue medication and refills on several occasions, leading to missed doses and a noticeable worsening of seizure control compared with earlier this year.

Context and Contributing Factors:

- The patient must request escorted access to lockers and pharmacy deliveries, often resulting in delays beyond dosing times.
- Meal breaks are occasionally curtailed or rescheduled at short notice during maintenance windows, coinciding with increased spasms on fasted days.
- No alcohol or recreational drug use reported; sleep is fragmented on night shifts.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Provide the patient with a written dosing schedule and a work letter requesting reasonable adjustments: guaranteed meal breaks aligned with dosing, timely escorted access to personal medication, and permission to carry a limited supply of non‑contaminating, sealed medication within permitted zones.
- Issue a duplicate supply card for community pharmacy to reduce refill delays and encourage use of a monitored dosage system to mitigate missed doses.
- Maintain current doses for now while the above adjustments are implemented; we will reassess frequency and severity at review in 8–10 weeks.
- If access and adherence improve but spasms persist, we will discuss alternative therapies at next clinic visit.

I have arranged follow‑up in the next available clinic. Please advise if workplace liaison documentation requires specific wording.

With best wishes,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

 Staffordshire ST5 5BG

Dear Dr Kate

Wendy Brown, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K482715 NHS No. 6592841037
Flat 7 Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset. Patient has practiced aura recognition strategies and is able to describe prodromal warning features in her own words.

Current concerns: She reports that over the past three months she has had brief clusters of events described as “jaw tightness then body goes stiff on the right, and I can’t get words out”, typically lasting under a minute with rapid recovery. She links these to missed doses of anti-seizure medication and has kept a diary demonstrating a temporal association. Overall seizure pattern is best characterised as focal tonic with missed ASM doses.

Aura/ictal description (patient-reported): “A rising wave in my stomach, a metallic taste, and sounds feel far away for a few seconds. If I catch it early and sit down, it sometimes fades; if I’ve missed tablets, it goes into the jaw clamping and right arm stiffening.” No clear generalisation reported. Postictal phase minimal; feels briefly foggy.

Relevant history: No new neurological deficits. No recent head injury. Sleep fragmented due to work shifts. Alcohol minimal. No pregnancy plans. Family history negative for epilepsy.

Investigations: Prior MRI (2016) reported no definite epileptogenic lesion. Routine EEG in 2019 showed intermittent left temporal slowing without epileptiform discharges. No recent bloods on file.

Medication: Lamotrigine modified-release 200 mg twice daily. Rescue: buccal midazolam 10 mg PRN prolonged events (not used in past year). Reports occasional missed evening doses during late shifts. No known drug allergies.

Self-management: She has actively used aura recognition strategies learned at previous epilepsy nurse sessions and keeps a structured seizure and dosing diary on her phone. She has set phone alarms but reports these are sometimes ignored during busy shifts.

Examination (today): Alert, oriented, fluent speech. Cranial nerves intact. Motor and coordination normal. No focal deficits. BP 118/72, pulse 72 regular.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Reinforce adherence: patient to switch to a single daily dosing schedule if suitable after serum level review; she agrees to pharmacy blister packs and watch-based reminders to reduce missed doses.
- Bloods requested: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, lamotrigine trough level in 1–2 weeks.
- Safety: reiterated individual seizure triggers (sleep loss, missed doses) and advised continued use of aura recognition strategies, sitting/lying down when warnings occur, and maintaining diary entries.
- Work: letter offered to occupational health to consider minor shift adjustments to support adherence.
- Follow-up: nurse telephone review in 6 weeks to review levels, adherence tools, and seizure diary; clinic review in 4 months or earlier if events increase or generalise.

Thank you for your continued care of Wendy Brown. Please contact me if there are any concerns or if adherence remains problematic despite the above measures.

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Aisha Rahman 
South Thames Neurology Centre 
Neurology Outpatients, 12 Kingfisher Way, London SE10 3AB 

Dear Dr Aisha Rahman, 

Michael Turner, DOB: 23-02-1991, Hospital No: H392715 NHS No. 9182736450 
Flat 7, 28 Willow Street, London EC2A 4BH 

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; prior nocturnal and daytime focal to bilateral tonic–clonic evolution reported in March

sections.name: current medication
sections.text:
Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily 
Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for clusters (not recently required)

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue levetiracetam at current dose 
- Maintain adherence supports (pillbox and phone reminders) 
- Safety advice reinforced; no driving until formally cleared per DVLA guidance 
- Routine follow-up in 6 months, sooner if any breakthrough events 

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports a sustained improvement in seizure control since adopting adherence supports (pillbox and smartphone reminders), which have markedly reduced missed doses and improved day-to-day stability. For context, before this improvement: in March he had 5 seizures during sleep and 5 while awake; in April he had none in sleep and one while awake. Since then, there have been no events suggestive of seizures, and he has not experienced aura-like symptoms, automatisms, or post-event confusion. He has also not required rescue clobazam. 

He describes satisfactory sleep, reduced stress at work, and consistent medication timing aided by the pillbox and reminders. Neurological examination today is unremarkable, and there are no adverse effects from levetiracetam. Based on the current trajectory and the absence of events, I have advised continuation of the present regimen and adherence measures. We discussed seizure first-aid and lifestyle precautions. He understands to contact us promptly if there is any recurrence or change in semiology. 

With best wishes,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Simon Patel
Maple Grove Health Centre

12 Maple Grove, Southwark, London SE1 4AB

Dear Dr Simon Patel

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-05-1990, Hospital No: K918372 NHS No. 7482106395
Flat 3, 22 Larch Court, Peckham, London SE15 2NL

Summary: Ongoing seizures of uncertain classification; prior normal MRI; high caffeine intake acknowledged.

Thank you for asking me to review Daniel Harris in clinic today. He attended alone and brought a seizure diary. He reports historically infrequent events since last year, with improved routine and sleep hygiene. He began Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily via primary care after a telephone consultation in December. He remained seizure-free for 8 months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure three Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences. He describes the absences as brief episodes of lost time with staring and no awareness, occurring over the preceding weekend. The generalised event occurred mid-morning at work; there was a tongue bite and post-ictal confusion for approximately 30 minutes. No injury was sustained.

He drinks high-caffeine energy drinks (3–4 cans most weekdays) and several strong coffees daily, particularly when working late. He acknowledges poor hydration and intermittent missed breakfasts. Sleep is reported as 5–6 hours on weeknights with weekend catch-up. No recent intercurrent illness. No new medications. He denies alcohol misuse or recreational drugs. There is no clear trigger other than increased workload and caffeine. Family history unremarkable for seizures.

Investigations: He recalls an MRI head from 2019 reported as normal (no report available today). No prior EEGs available in our system. Routine bloods earlier this year were reportedly normal via GP.

Current medication: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily; Vitamin D 1,000 IU daily (self-purchased). No known drug allergies.

Adverse effects: Mild irritability in the first fortnight of Levetiracetam, settled without intervention. No rash, diplopia, ataxia or weight change reported.

Driving and safety: We discussed DVLA requirements around seizures and the need to cease driving and inform DVLA following the recent event; he states he has already stopped driving and will notify DVLA. We also reviewed general safety measures at work and at home (showers rather than baths, supervised swimming, and awareness for colleagues).

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue Levetiracetam at current dose for now; adherence reinforced. We will review the seizure diary over the next 6–8 weeks.
- Lifestyle measures: he agreed to reduce caffeine substantially, improve hydration, and regularise sleep and meals; these were framed as patient-led goals.
- Arrange: routine EEG and repeat MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) to update baseline investigations.
- Blood tests via GP: FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D, and Levetiracetam trough level for reference.
- Nurse specialist telephone review in 4–6 weeks to assess seizure control, side effects, and progress with caffeine reduction and sleep routine.
- Follow-up in clinic in 3 months, sooner if further events occur.

Thank you for your ongoing care of Daniel Harris. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Simon Patel
Maple Grove Health Centre

12 Maple Grove, Southwark, London SE1 4AB

Dear Dr Simon Patel

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-05-1990, Hospital No: K918372 NHS No. 7482106395
Flat 3, 22 Larch Court, Peckham, London SE15 2NL

Summary: Ongoing seizures of uncertain classification; prior normal MRI; high caffeine intake acknowledged.

Thank you for asking me to review Daniel Harris in clinic today. He attended alone and brought a seizure diary. He reports historically infrequent events since last year, with improved routine and sleep hygiene. He began Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily via primary care after a telephone consultation in December. He remained seizure-free for 8 months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure three Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences. He describes the absences as brief episodes of lost time with staring and no awareness, occurring over the preceding weekend. The generalised event occurred mid-morning at work; there was a tongue bite and post-ictal confusion for approximately 30 minutes. No injury was sustained.

He drinks high-caffeine energy drinks (3–4 cans most weekdays) and several strong coffees daily, particularly when working late. He acknowledges poor hydration and intermittent missed breakfasts. Sleep is reported as 5–6 hours on weeknights with weekend catch-up. No recent intercurrent illness. No new medications. He denies alcohol misuse or recreational drugs. There is no clear trigger other than increased workload and caffeine. Family history unremarkable for seizures.

Investigations: He recalls an MRI head from 2019 reported as normal (no report available today). No prior EEGs available in our system. Routine bloods earlier this year were reportedly normal via GP.

Current medication: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily; Vitamin D 1,000 IU daily (self-purchased). No known drug allergies.

Adverse effects: Mild irritability in the first fortnight of Levetiracetam, settled without intervention. No rash, diplopia, ataxia or weight change reported.

Driving and safety: We discussed DVLA requirements around seizures and the need to cease driving and inform DVLA following the recent event; he states he has already stopped driving and will notify DVLA. We also reviewed general safety measures at work and at home (showers rather than baths, supervised swimming, and awareness for colleagues).

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue Levetiracetam at current dose for now; adherence reinforced. We will review the seizure diary over the next 6–8 weeks.
- Lifestyle measures: he agreed to reduce caffeine substantially, improve hydration, and regularise sleep and meals; these were framed as patient-led goals.
- Arrange: routine EEG and repeat MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) to update baseline investigations.
- Blood tests via GP: FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D, and Levetiracetam trough level for reference.
- Nurse specialist telephone review in 4–6 weeks to assess seizure control, side effects, and progress with caffeine reduction and sleep routine.
- Follow-up in clinic in 3 months, sooner if further events occur.

Thank you for your ongoing care of Daniel Harris. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 27 September 2012 

Dr Amelia Rhodes 
South Thames Neurology Centre 
Neurology Outpatients, 14 Kingfisher Way, London SE10 3LX 

Dear Dr Amelia Rhodes, 

Emily Carter, DOB: 23-02-1986, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9045127783 
Flat 5, 27 Brookfield Terrace, London, N4 2JP 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification with increasing frequency and situational sensitivity.

sections.name: current medication
sections.text:
Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily 
Clonazepam 0.5 mg nocte as rescue for clusters (patient-reported, used sparingly)

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Increase Levetiracetam to 750 mg twice daily and monitor tolerability and mood 
- Provide written seizure first-aid advice and workplace adjustments letter (to address lighting triggers) 
- Arrange urgent EEG and MRI brain with epilepsy protocol 
- Bloods today: U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D; Levetiracetam level if clinically indicated 
- Safety counselling: driving, machinery, heights, water, and sleep hygiene 
- Follow-up in 6 weeks or earlier if further deterioration 

I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today following a clear deterioration in her seizure control over recent months. She has started a new role in an open-plan office where strong and intermittently flickering lighting is present, and she has noticed more events in that environment. As of this month she reports 2 seizure events; two in August, 7 in July and 2 in June during both sleep and wakefulness. Events are described as brief episodes of behavioural arrest with loss of awareness, sometimes preceded by a rising internal sensation and flashing lights in her vision, occasionally progressing to bilateral stiffening and jerking lasting under two minutes, with post-event confusion and headache. There is no tongue biting or urinary incontinence reported in the majority of episodes. Her partner has witnessed two daytime collapses at work, both temporally associated with exposure to overhead flicker. Sleep deprivation and stress appear to exacerbate frequency. 

There is no history of recent head injury, fever, or new systemic illness. She does not consume alcohol to excess and denies recreational drug use. Adherence to current medication is good by pill count and patient report. Neurological examination today is non-focal. 

Given the pattern of escalation and the environmental trigger at the new workplace, I have recommended an increase in Levetiracetam to 750 mg twice daily, provision of an employer letter requesting adjustments (reduced flicker/strong light exposure, screen filters, breaks, and option to work in a lower-stimulus area), and urgent neurophysiological and imaging reassessment. We discussed risks related to driving and safety-critical tasks; she is aware of the current guidance and will refrain accordingly. Written seizure first-aid information has been supplied. 

I will review her in six weeks, sooner if there are further clusters, injuries, or any emergence of mood or behavioural adverse effects. Please do not hesitate to contact me if there are concerns in the interim. 

With best wishes,

I will review her in six weeks, sooner if there are further clusters, injuries, or any emergence of mood or behavioural adverse effects. Please do not hesitate to contact me if there are concerns in the interim. 

With best wishes,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Hannah Keane
St Bartholomew’s Hospital
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE

Dear Dr Hannah Keane,

Liam Turner, DOB: 14-03-2003, Hospital No: H932774 NHS No. 4098573216
12 Alder Court, Mile End, London E3 4HT

Diagnoses:

Recurrent seizure episodes of uncertain classification (onset 2023)
Normal brain MRI (January 2024)

sections.name: current medication
sections.text: Levetiracetam 500 mg bd

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
Optimise sleep hygiene and reduce prolonged continuous screen exposure
Continue Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily and review in 6 months
Provide seizure safety advice and sports participation guidance

I reviewed Liam Turner in clinic today. He is a university esports club lead and reports prolonged screen time due to training, competition schedules, and event administration. He describes brief events characterised by a rising epigastric sensation, momentary déjà vu, and preserved awareness, occasionally followed by mild right-hand tingling without spread. He reports a frequency of ≤ three simple partial seizures per week, usually clustering during weeks with late-night tournaments and irregular sleep. There are no witnessed convulsions, tongue biting, or incontinence. Post-event fatigue is mild and short-lived.

Since starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily in May 2024, he notes a reduction in intensity and duration of episodes, though frequency remains tied to sleep disruption and extended sessions at dual monitors with high visual load. He denies mood change or irritability on current dose. No alcohol misuse or recreational drugs. He maintains academic attendance and coaching responsibilities.

Neurological examination today is normal. Prior MRI brain (January 2024) was reported as normal. A routine EEG (February 2024) was non-diagnostic. We discussed the pattern of triggers, particularly irregular sleep and prolonged continuous screen time with limited breaks. He will implement protected sleep windows, scheduled screen breaks (every 45–60 minutes), blue-light reduction settings in the evenings, and avoidance of overnight events where possible. He will track events alongside sleep duration and training intensity to help correlate triggers.

I have advised continuation of Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily. We agreed to reassess in 6 months, earlier if there is any evolution in semiology, loss of awareness, injury, or generalisation. He has been provided with seizure first-aid information for teammates and guidance on safe participation in esports tournaments and travel planning. DVLA guidance was discussed with respect to awareness-preserving events; he currently reports preserved awareness and no events at the wheel, and he does not drive.

I would be grateful if you could continue prescribing Levetiracetam 500 mg bd. Please let me know if there are any concerns regarding mood or adherence. I remain happy to review sooner should the pattern change or if he wishes to consider ambulatory EEG during tournament periods.

With best wishes,

sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Hannah Reid (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 02 October 2025 10:15
To: kch.neuroepilepsy@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Naveen Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Emily Grant (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Interval review and VNS usage update

Hi Hannah

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Primary generalised epilepsy

Present Medication:

1. Valproate (modified-release) 500 mg in the morning and 1000 mg at night
2. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for clusters (per personalised plan)
3. Vitamin D3 1000 IU once daily

sections.name: present seizure frequency
sections.text:
Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every 4 days. Rescue medication was required once in June and twice in August. The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review.

Context and Patient-Reported Features:

- The patient has generalised epilepsy with occasional cluster patterns linked to sleep loss, particularly after late shifts or long-haul travel. 
- A personalised plan for cluster management is documented elsewhere and remains unchanged; the patient is familiar with when to initiate the rescue protocol and when to present to emergency services.
- Vagus nerve stimulator in situ since 2023; the patient reports more frequent magnet swiping at aura onset and during early cluster periods. No adverse effects beyond transient voice change. Battery status checked at last device clinic: satisfactory.
- No recent hospital admissions. Headaches post-cluster respond to simple analgesia and hydration.
- The patient is exploring sleep hygiene strategies (fixed bedtime, reduction of caffeine after midday) and has begun using a sleep diary to identify triggers.

Plan of Action:

- Continue current anti-seizure regimen without dose changes at this time, given stable overall frequency and effective use of the personalised rescue plan.
- Reinforce sleep optimisation and adherence to the personalised plan. Patient to maintain seizure and sleep diary to better delineate the relationship with sleep loss and cluster timing.
- No change to VNS parameters today; continue magnet use at aura onset and during clusters as per training. Next VNS device check at routine device clinic in 3 months or sooner if concerns.
- Safety advice reiterated regarding water precautions, heights, and operating machinery. The patient confirms understanding and has support at home.
- Follow-up in 4 months, or earlier if there is any increase in seizure severity, prolonged clusters, adverse effects, or concerns about VNS function.

With best wishes

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Emily Carter (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 14 April 2015 10:22
To: epilepsy.clinic@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Ravi Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Helen Brooks (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Worsening seizures, medication review and safety advice

Dear Emily,

Summary and current concerns:
I reviewed Sam in clinic today. Over the last two months his seizure control has clearly deteriorated. He reports increasing social pressures at work and more frequent evenings out, with higher alcohol exposure than previously, which he recognises often precedes poor sleep and clusters of events.

Seizure history and current frequency:
He keeps a diary. In Feb he had four seizures during sleep and 3 while awake. In Mar he had 4 in sleep and 2 while awake. Prior to this, he had gone several months with only occasional brief episodes, typically after late nights.

Current medicines:
- Carbamazepine modified‑release 400 mg twice daily
- Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily

Previous medicines (patient‑reported):
- Lamotrigine up to 100 mg twice daily (stopped in 2014 due to rash)
- Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily (self‑discontinued in 2013 because of weight gain and tremor)

Examination and collateral:
Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Mood is strained; he reports sleep restriction, variable adherence when out socially, and alcohol intake most weekends. No new focal neurological symptoms between events.

sections.name: assessment
sections.text: Sam’s seizure burden has increased in the context of heightened social stressors, reduced sleep, variable adherence, and increased alcohol exposure. He feels the recent uptitration of levetiracetam may have coincided with more frequent events and irritability, though this is confounded by lifestyle factors. There is no single reliable non‑medication trigger identified beyond alcohol and sleep loss.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Medication: Maintain carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily for now. Reduce levetiracetam to 500 mg twice daily over one week to assess for behavioural side effects while monitoring seizure frequency. Begin lacosamide 50 mg at night for one week, then 50 mg twice daily ongoing. If tolerated and seizures remain frequent, we will consider further titration at the next review.
- Safety and lifestyle: Strongly advised strict alcohol moderation/avoidance, consistent sleep schedule, and adherence with dosing alarms. Discussed interim driving and safety precautions given recent events, including avoiding swimming alone, working at heights, and operating machinery.
- Monitoring: Sam will continue his seizure diary, noting alcohol, sleep duration, and doses taken. We will check U&E/LFTs in four weeks and review in clinic in eight weeks, sooner if events escalate.

I will update you after the medication adjustment period. Please contact me if there are concerns before then.

With best wishes,

sections.text:
- Medication: Maintain carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily for now. Reduce levetiracetam to 500 mg twice daily over one week to assess for behavioural side effects while monitoring seizure frequency. Begin lacosamide 50 mg at night for one week, then 50 mg twice daily ongoing. If tolerated and seizures remain frequent, we will consider further titration at the next review.
- Safety and lifestyle: Strongly advised strict alcohol moderation/avoidance, consistent sleep schedule, and adherence with dosing alarms. Discussed interim driving and safety precautions given recent events, including avoiding swimming alone, working at heights, and operating machinery.
- Monitoring: Sam will continue his seizure diary, noting alcohol, sleep duration, and doses taken. We will check U&E/LFTs in four weeks and review in clinic in eight weeks, sooner if events escalate.

I will update you after the medication adjustment period. Please contact me if there are concerns before then.

With best wishes,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Alicia Morton 
Riverside Medical Practice 
12 Wharf Street, London SE8 3DR 

Dear Dr Alicia Morton, 

Jordan Ellis, DOB: 22-07-1989, Hospital No: U918273 NHS No. 3049185562 
Flat 5, 28 Fairview Road, London N7 8HP 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with ongoing seizures; history of migraine; mild anxiety symptoms; no known drug allergies; lives with partner; non-smoker; minimal alcohol intake. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily. 
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily. 
Sumatriptan 50 mg as required for migraine (max 2 doses in 24 hours). 
Sertraline 50 mg once daily. 

I reviewed Jordan Ellis in clinic today. They attended alone and provided data from a seizure diary app, which they have been using consistently for the past four months. The app includes timestamped entries, short free-text descriptions, and prompts for potential triggers. The patient reports good adherence to medication with no recent missed doses. Sleep remains irregular due to shift work, and they are working with their employer to stabilise schedules. No recent head injuries, intercurrent illness, or new medications. Appetite is normal, and weight is stable.

Seizures: The app logs indicate a regular pattern of seizures twice every two weeks, typically clustering on days following consecutive late shifts. Episodes are described as brief events with preserved awareness followed by fatigue and mild headache; there have been no injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence reported. Partners’ observations, entered into the app on shared entries, corroborate the timing and brief duration. No rescue medication has been required. The patient’s last emergency department attendance for a seizure-related event was over a year ago. 

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. No focal neurological deficits. Gait normal. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable.

sections.name: investigations
sections.text:
- Routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) from August 2025 were within normal limits. 
- Lamotrigine and levetiracetam levels not checked recently. 
- MRI brain (2023) unchanged from prior imaging; no acute pathology. 
- EEG (2022) was non-specific; no clear epileptiform discharges captured.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Continue current anti-seizure medications unchanged for now. 
- Request trough levels for lamotrigine and levetiracetam at the GP practice to assess for subtherapeutic range in the context of ongoing events. 
- Encourage ongoing seizure diary app use; patient to enable automatic export so we can review monthly trends. 
- Provide fit note advice as needed; patient to avoid working consecutive late shifts if possible and to discuss reasonable adjustments with occupational health. 
- Standard safety counselling reinforced (showers rather than baths, avoid heights, cook with caution). 
- Follow-up in clinic in 4 months, or sooner if there is an increase beyond the current pattern of seizures twice every two weeks or any event associated with injury or loss of awareness. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 20 November 2021

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; history of traumatic brain injury; travel-related anxiety; osteoporosis; former alcohol dependence (in sustained remission); hypertension; hyperlipidaemia. Currently residing in supported accommodation. Smoker.

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Sarah Coleman 
Consultant Neurologist 
Centre for Epilepsy 
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr Sarah Coleman, 

Alex Turner, DOB: 22-08-1992, Hospital No: K847392 NHS No. 1039485726 
Flat 12, 4 Brookfield Road, London, N16 7HF 

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; phenotype under ongoing evaluation

Aetiology: 

No clear structural, metabolic, or genetic cause identified to date

Other Medical Problems: 

Generalised anxiety symptoms under community psychological support; mild iron-deficiency anaemia treated with oral ferrous fumarate

Management Plan: 

Continue current anti-seizure medicines: Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily and Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily. Maintain single-brand supply for both. Arrange serum levels and U&Es/LFTs prior to next review. Safety advice reinforced around nocturnal events and supervision overnight when feasible. Sleep hygiene and regular routines encouraged. Continue seizure and mood diary. Review in three months, sooner if escalation occurs. 

History and Progress: 

Since the last appointment, the patient reports three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~4 - 5 events. These are brief, stereotyped episodes occurring typically between 02:00 and 04:00, with rapid post-event reorientation. No clear daytime events were reported. The patient notes prodromal mood change and irritability for 12–24 hours prior to some clusters and has been tracking this in a diary, which shows a temporal association with sleep deprivation and increased work-related stress. No new head injuries, intercurrent infections, or alcohol binges reported. 

Medication Tolerability and Past Treatments: 

Previously trialled Carbamazepine (dizziness and diplopia at therapeutic doses) and Topiramate (word-finding difficulties and paraesthesia), both discontinued. Currently tolerating Levetiracetam and Lacosamide with manageable irritability that appears to fluctuate with sleep and stress rather than dose changes. No rash, hyponatraemia symptoms, or weight loss reported. Adherence reported as good; pharmacy records corroborate on monthly collection.

sections.name: investigations
sections.text:
Prior MRI brain (2024) unremarkable. Routine EEG (2024) non-diagnostic. Ambulatory home EEG has been requested to better capture nocturnal events and correlate with the diary; awaiting scheduling. Baseline bloods (June 2025) normal apart from mild iron deficiency now treated. 

Lifestyle and Safety: 

We discussed SUDEP and general safety; the patient uses a phone-based seizure alert app at night and has arranged for a family member to check in when clusters occur. Driving remains not applicable as the patient does not hold a licence. Work arrangements have been modified to avoid night shifts and mitigate sleep loss.

Plan Summary: 

- Continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg BD and Lacosamide 100 mg BD (single-brand). 
- Pre-clinic bloods: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, Levetiracetam and Lacosamide levels. 
- Proceed with ambulatory EEG to characterise nocturnal clusters. 
- Maintain detailed seizure and mood/sleep diary; bring to next review. 
- Review in three months in clinic; earlier contact if frequency increases or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

sections.text:
Prior MRI brain (2024) unremarkable. Routine EEG (2024) non-diagnostic. Ambulatory home EEG has been requested to better capture nocturnal events and correlate with the diary; awaiting scheduling. Baseline bloods (June 2025) normal apart from mild iron deficiency now treated. 

Lifestyle and Safety: 

We discussed SUDEP and general safety; the patient uses a phone-based seizure alert app at night and has arranged for a family member to check in when clusters occur. Driving remains not applicable as the patient does not hold a licence. Work arrangements have been modified to avoid night shifts and mitigate sleep loss.

Plan Summary: 

- Continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg BD and Lacosamide 100 mg BD (single-brand). 
- Pre-clinic bloods: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, Levetiracetam and Lacosamide levels. 
- Proceed with ambulatory EEG to characterise nocturnal clusters. 
- Maintain detailed seizure and mood/sleep diary; bring to next review. 
- Review in three months in clinic; earlier contact if frequency increases or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: body
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 11 December 2015 

Dr Anita Shah
Riverside Medical Practice

12 Riverside Way, London, SE1 9ZZ
Dear Dr Anita Shah
Michael Carter, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: M827461 NHS No. 8845123367
Flat 4, 29 Millstream Court, London, SE16 3LP 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since his last appointment there has been a notable deterioration in his condition. He reports recent job loss from his banking role, with associated stress and disrupted sleep patterns over several weeks. In the 24 hours prior to clinic he experienced multiple seizures in past day, described by him and his partner as brief episodes of loss of awareness with post-event confusion and marked fatigue. He also reports several near-miss episodes over the preceding week, including sudden staring and unresponsiveness lasting under a minute, followed by headaches and irritability.

He is currently taking Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. and Clobazam 10 mg nocte, both of which he states he has taken regularly. He denies missed doses but admits that sleep fragmentation and early waking have worsened his daytime somnolence and concentration. He also notes increased anxiety since becoming unemployed, with occasional palpitations and a sense of impending events before the episodes. There have been no clear triggers such as intercurrent infection or alcohol excess, though caffeine intake has risen. No injuries were reported, but there is concern from family regarding safety at home given the recent escalation.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and cranial nerves. Motor and coordination were intact; no focal neurological deficits were identified. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. BP in clinic was mildly elevated, likely stress-related.

Given the clinical picture and the clear pattern of worsening events temporally associated with stress and sleep disruption, I have advised close observation and prompt contact if events cluster or if any convulsive episodes occur. I have arranged urgent ambulatory EEG within the next 1–2 weeks and will request updated serum drug levels and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC). He has been provided seizure first-aid guidance for his partner and we discussed practical risk mitigation at home. He is aware of the DVLA guidance and understands he should not drive. I will review him in an expedited follow-up once the ambulatory EEG is completed.

Yours sincerely
