sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 15 January 2019

Dr Eleanor Hughes
St Cuthbert Neurology Centre
Neurology Outpatients, 12 Morley Lane, Leeds LS2 8AB

Dear Dr Eleanor Hughes,

Michael Turner, DOB: 03-02-1991, Hospital No: L937520 NHS No. 9054312768
22 Brookside Close, Leeds, LS7 3HT

Diagnoses:

Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness seizures; prior MRI unremarkable; interictal EEG previously normal.

Current Management:

Ketogenic/modified diet (supervised by an external specialist dietetic team); diet details recorded in notes. No regular anti-seizure medicines at present.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
Continue current dietary therapy with ongoing external dietetic supervision and ensure copies of dietary reviews are shared with our service. Safety advice reinforced. Routine bloods (lipid profile, liver function, bicarbonate) via GP every 6 months while on diet. Review in neurology clinic in 12 months, or sooner if events recur or dietary intolerance emerges.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports a history consistent with focal epilepsy, characterised previously by brief episodes of behavioural arrest with automatisms and post‑event confusion lasting a few minutes. He has been following a clinician‑supervised ketogenic/modified diet for several months; the dietary protocol and ratios have been documented in the medical record. Since adopting the diet, he describes that seizure occurrences have not been happening, and there have been no witnessed episodes by family or colleagues. He denies new neurological symptoms. Sleep and hydration are reported as stable. He is not currently taking lamotrigine or other anti‑seizure medicines.

On examination today he was well, with normal neurological findings. We discussed ongoing safety measures, driving regulations where applicable, and the importance of maintaining meticulous dietary adherence and regular monitoring while on this therapy. I have asked that the external dietetic team continue to supervise macronutrient ratios and provide periodic summaries for our records. Should any breakthrough focal events occur, we would reconsider pharmacological options and repeat EEG.

I have arranged follow‑up in 12 months. He may contact the clinic earlier if there is any change in seizure pattern, adverse effects from the diet, or other concerns.

With best wishes,

Current Management:

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
Continue current dietary therapy with ongoing external dietetic supervision and ensure copies of dietary reviews are shared with our service. Safety advice reinforced. Routine bloods (lipid profile, liver function, bicarbonate) via GP every 6 months while on diet. Review in neurology clinic in 12 months, or sooner if events recur or dietary intolerance emerges.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports a history consistent with focal epilepsy, characterised previously by brief episodes of behavioural arrest with automatisms and post‑event confusion lasting a few minutes. He has been following a clinician‑supervised ketogenic/modified diet for several months; the dietary protocol and ratios have been documented in the medical record. Since adopting the diet, he describes that seizure occurrences have not been happening, and there have been no witnessed episodes by family or colleagues. He denies new neurological symptoms. Sleep and hydration are reported as stable. He is not currently taking lamotrigine or other anti‑seizure medicines.

On examination today he was well, with normal neurological findings. We discussed ongoing safety measures, driving regulations where applicable, and the importance of maintaining meticulous dietary adherence and regular monitoring while on this therapy. I have asked that the external dietetic team continue to supervise macronutrient ratios and provide periodic summaries for our records. Should any breakthrough focal events occur, we would reconsider pharmacological options and repeat EEG.

I have arranged follow‑up in 12 months. He may contact the clinic earlier if there is any change in seizure pattern, adverse effects from the diet, or other concerns.

With best wishes,

sections.name: body
sections.text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 22 November 2024 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist 
King's College Hospital 
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist, 

Daniel Fraser, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH928374 NHS No. 9432175609 
12 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 3QA

Thank you for asking me to assess this patient, who attended today with his partner. He reports intermittent episodes over several years characterised by a brief prodrome of tightness across the chest, a rising internal "surge", and an abrupt loss of awareness witnessed by his partner. On most occasions there is bilateral hand fumbling and a brief vocalisation. He does not recall the episodes and is usually confused for 10–15 minutes afterwards, occasionally with a dull bifrontal headache. He denies tongue biting or incontinence. There is no clear motor convulsion described. He keeps a diary and, based on this, the episodes are now occurring abs monthly, with intervening weeks free of events. He links recent clustering to periods of poor sleep and heavy workload.

Of note, he acknowledges high caffeine intake. He typically consumes four to five large coffees by midday and often an energy drink during late meetings. He and his partner have noticed that on days with very high caffeine use and reduced sleep, he feels more jittery and has experienced the prodromal "surge" without progression on two occasions. He has reduced alcohol substantially and reports no recreational drug use. He does not drive. There is no family history of similar episodes. He had a normal birth and development and no history of significant head injury or CNS infection. His medications include ramipril for hypertension and vitamin D. He is not on any antiseizure medication currently.

Previous investigations: routine EEG one year ago reportedly normal; MRI brain without contrast five years ago for headaches was described as normal. I have not yet reviewed the original images or tracings. Cardiovascular review is unremarkable; no syncope, palpitations, or exertional chest pain. Neurological examination today was normal, including cranial nerves, motor, coordination, and gait. Mood appears generally stable; he describes situational anxiety around work deadlines but no persistent low mood, psychotic features, or panic attacks. Sleep is fragmented during busy periods (often <6 hours).

Overall, there are paroxysmal episodes with impaired awareness and post-event confusion. The semiology is not sufficiently specific to assign a definitive classification at this stage. Given the current pattern abs monthly, further characterisation is required to guide management and risk counselling. I have discussed prolonged EEG monitoring with home ambulatory recording as a first step, followed by inpatient video-EEG if required. He is agreeable to this plan. I will also arrange an MRI brain with epilepsy protocol to ensure high-resolution temporal lobe coverage, and I have requested thyroid function and metabolic profile via his GP to exclude contributory systemic factors.

In discussion, he independently raised concern about his caffeine habits. He will keep a simple log of daily caffeine amounts and sleep duration alongside his existing event diary so that we can correlate potential precipitants with episodes. We have also provided standard seizure first-aid information to his partner. No treatment changes were initiated today pending further data.

I will review him in clinic after the ambulatory EEG and MRI have been completed and will update you with the outcomes. Please let me know if there are interim concerns or if additional information from prior investigations becomes available.

Yours sincerely

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist

Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist, 

Daniel Fraser, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH928374 NHS No. 9432175609 
12 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 3QA

He reports intermittent episodes over several years characterised by a brief prodrome of tightness across the chest, a rising internal "surge", and an abrupt loss of awareness witnessed by his partner. On most occasions there is bilateral hand fumbling and a brief vocalisation. There is no clear motor convulsion described. He keeps a diary and, based on this, the episodes are now occurring abs monthly, with intervening weeks free of events. He links recent clustering to periods of poor sleep and heavy workload.

Of note, he acknowledges high caffeine intake. He typically consumes four to five large coffees by midday and often an energy drink during late meetings. He and his partner have noticed that on days with very high caffeine use and reduced sleep, he feels more jittery and has experienced the prodromal "surge" without progression on two occasions. He has reduced alcohol substantially and reports no recreational drug use. He does not drive. There is no family history of similar episodes. He had a normal birth and development and no history of significant head injury or CNS infection. His medications include ramipril for hypertension and vitamin D. He is not on any antiseizure medication currently.

Mood appears generally stable; he describes situational anxiety around work deadlines but no persistent low mood, psychotic features, or panic attacks. Sleep is fragmented during busy periods (often <6 hours).

Overall, there are paroxysmal episodes with impaired awareness and post-event confusion. The semiology is not sufficiently specific to assign a definitive classification at this stage. Given the current pattern abs monthly, further characterisation is required to guide management and risk counselling. I have discussed prolonged EEG monitoring with home ambulatory recording as a first step, followed by inpatient video-EEG if required. He is agreeable to this plan.
